It was a quiet June day in Genoa, Wisconsin, until a call for a welfare check would soon turn into a violent confrontation for these officers. What happened next would also be controversial, as questions swirled as to whether officers met the legal threshold to detain the subject prior to the tragic incident. Officers were called to check on 61-year-old William Boardman, who had been showing signs of severe paranoia worsened by meth use. His daughter said they hadn't seen him since 2022, and he had been unstable and erratic with worsening mental health. They said his escalating meth use led to hallucinations, bizarre behavior, and fits of rage where he damaged the home. Under these increasingly tense circumstances, William was about to meet Deputy Bradley Brugman, and what followed would leave the small town forever changed.

Despite the deputy's attempts to perform first aid and the assistance of EMS, Boardman was pronounced dead at the scene. A month-long investigation by the Wisconsin DOJ included a 316-page report with interviews, videos, dispatch recordings, and forensic evidence. The Vernon County District Attorney eventually concluded that Deputy Brown's actions were justified and no criminal charges would be brought against him. This incident is part of a longer story with Boardman. According to his daughter, he had suffered from severe paranoia and worsening mental health, made worse by escalating meth use. He hadn't been seen by his family since December 2022, and his daughter had called that day for a welfare check, hoping to get him committed to a hospital. Family said Boardman's life had taken a dark turn after the deaths of both his parents in fire-related incidents at the same house, which family said triggered his mental decline. His drug use escalated and led to erratic and destructive behavior, including hallucinations and fits of rage. MN Crime has followed this case for the past 14 months, reviewing hundreds of investigative files and submitting numerous open records requests as part of our commitment to covering crime and police response in the rural Midwest.
